description: When the Warden requests changes, the pipeline loops back to Smith for another iteration. Currently Smith starts fresh with just the original bead description — it does not receive the Warden's specific feedback about what was wrong. Pass the Warden's review comments as structured context in the Smith prompt for iteration 2+. The prompt builder already has access to iteration count; extend it to accept warden feedback text. This should significantly improve fix rates on retry iterations since Smith will know exactly what the Warden objected to instead of guessing.
title: Warden feedback passthrough: give Smith structured context on retry

description: Bellows workers (CI fix, review fix, rebase) don't show their status in the Hearth TUI Workers panel. When bellows is actively working on a PR (rebasing, fixing CI, etc.), there's no visibility into what it's doing or how long it's been running. Add bellows worker entries to the Workers panel showing: bead ID, PR number, action (rebase/ci-fix/review-fix), duration, and current status.
title: Hearth: show bellows worker status in Workers panel

description: Currently adding or removing anvils in forge.yaml requires a daemon restart. The poller reads d.cfg.Load() each cycle so it would see new anvils, but bellows and depcheck monitors are initialized with a fixed anvilPaths map at startup. The hot-reload callback should update bellows monitor paths and depcheck scanner paths when anvils change. The applyChanges function already detects anvil count changes but only logs a warning.
title: Hot-reload: support adding and removing anvils without daemon restart

description: When grouping by anvil, anvils with no open/ready beads are hidden. They should still appear with (0) so the operator knows the repo is registered.
title: Hearth Queue: show all anvils even when empty (0)

description: Copilot CLI now supports --output-format json which emits JSONL events similar to Claude's stream-json. Currently Forge treats copilot as PlainText format, meaning no token extraction or structured output parsing. Update provider.go Format() to return StreamJSON for copilot, update copilotArgs() to pass --output-format json instead of -s/--silent, and verify readStreamJSON can parse copilot's JSONL events (field names may differ slightly from Claude — investigate and adapt). This enables token counting and cost estimation for copilot runs.
title: Copilot output format: switch from PlainText to StreamJSON (JSONL)

description: The Smith prompt template (internal/prompt/prompt.go) and AGENTS.md have no guidance on when a smith should signal it needs human help. Currently the only paths to needs_human are: Warden detecting no-diff, Schematic returning ActionClarify, and retry exhaustion. Smiths themselves should be instructed to explicitly flag when they encounter: ambiguous requirements they cannot resolve with reasonable interpretation, missing configuration or secrets they cannot access, external dependencies or URLs they need but do not have, architectural decisions that require human judgment. Update the default prompt template to include a section explaining how and when to escalate. The smith can signal this by exiting with a specific message pattern that the pipeline detects (e.g. NEEDS_HUMAN: reason), or by writing to a conventions file. Coordinate with how pipeline.go currently handles outcomes.
title: Smith prompt: add escalation instructions for needs_human flagging

close_reason: Already implemented and verified with tests (clarified error message with 0=critical-only hint)
description:
PR #14 (config.go line 202): the validation was updated to allow 0 as a valid value for auto_dispatch_min_priority when auto_dispatch is 'priority', but the error message no longer explains what 0 means operationally.

Add a hint in the error message, e.g.: 'auto_dispatch_min_priority must be 0-4 (0 = critical-only) when auto_dispatch is priority' so users can make an informed choice without reading source code.
title: Config: improve auto_dispatch_min_priority validation error message to clarify 0=critical-only

description: Temper's detectSteps() only checks for package.json at the worktree root. Hybrid projects like Hytte have Go at root and Node in web/. Temper should detect package.json in common subdirectories (web/, frontend/, client/) and run npm lint/build with the correct working directory.
title: Temper: detect Node projects in subdirectories (e.g. web/package.json)

description: Temper currently runs only go build, go vet, and go test for Go projects. Add golangci-lint as an optional step (skip if binary not found). golangci-lint catches a wide range of issues: unused code, error handling, style, complexity, security patterns. Add it after vet, before test. Make it configurable per-anvil via .forge/temper.yaml or forge.yaml anvil settings so repos can opt out.
title: Add golangci-lint to Temper Go checks

description: Modify the Hearth TUI (internal/hearth) to split the center 'Workers' panel into two vertical rows. The top row should show the list of active workers, and the bottom row should show a 'Live Activity' log for the currently selected worker by parsing the stream-json events from the worker's log file (e.g., Tool calls, Thinking deltas). Use lipgloss.JoinVertical for the split.
title: Hearth: Split-view Worker Activity Log

description: When warden returns VerdictReject because Smith produced no diff, pipeline.Run() sets WorkerFailed and returns immediately. The bead stays in_progress with no active worker or branch, requiring manual reset. This is the same class of issue as the rate-limit stuck-bead (Forge-rpc) but triggered by a different code path. Fix options: (1) no-diff should be treated as a human-attention case, not a hard failure — flag the bead with needs_human=1 and reset to open, (2) if pipeline detects no-diff on first iteration it could retry with a clarified prompt, (3) at minimum the daemon's pipeline error handler must reset the bead to open when outcome.Error is non-nil (the rate-limit fix in Forge-rpc likely covers this too — verify). Observed: 09:56:26 warden_reject [Forge-h7x] No changes detected — Smith produced no diff; bead remained in_progress.
title: Pipeline/Warden: VerdictReject (no-diff) leaves bead stuck in_progress

description: GroupComments() in learn.go only groups comments with identical normalized body text. Copilot comments about the same pattern but with different wording (e.g. 'missing error check on Open()' vs 'error from ReadFile not handled') create separate rules instead of being grouped. Consider using Claude to cluster semantically similar comments before distillation, or use a simpler heuristic like keyword overlap scoring.
title: Warden learn: semantic comment grouping instead of exact-match

description:
PR #6 and PR #14 both flag duplicate rendering work in the event panel:

1. scrollDown() calls renderAllEventLines() just to compute len(allLines) for scroll bounds. Since View()/renderEvents() also calls renderAllEventLines(), every scroll keypress triggers the full wrap/render pass twice.

2. renderAllEventLines() calls renderEventLines() twice per event: once to determine selection mapping and once to append lines. Each call does wrapping and string allocation.

Fix: cache the flattened rendered-line slice for the current terminal width and invalidate on width change or new events. Alternatively, add a lightweight lineCount(event, width) helper that avoids string allocation so scrollDown can get the count without a full render.
title: Hearth: eliminate redundant renderAllEventLines calls in scrollDown and renderEventLines

description:
Before the depcheck scanner creates a bead for a dependency update, it must check:

1. `bd list --status=open --json` + `bd list --status=in_progress --json` — search for beads with matching package name in title/description
2. Open PRs in state DB — check if any open PR's bead references the package
3. Recently closed beads (last 7 days) — avoid re-creating beads that were just intentionally closed

Use consistent title convention `Deps(<ecosystem>): update <package> <old> → <new>` for reliable dedup matching.

Shared function: `DedupCheck(ctx, anvilPath, packageName, ecosystem) bool` usable by all ecosystem scanners.
title: Depcheck: deduplication — check existing beads and PRs before creating update beads

description: IsCrucibleCandidate and lookupEpicBranch filter on issue_type=='epic', but feature-type beads can also have children and should be eligible for crucible orchestration. close_reason: Added explicit prohibition section to both AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md: never start local dolt sql-server on port 3306, always use kubectl port-forward to tn-heimdall/dolt-beads. Committed in 0f0972f.
description: Forge's beads DB connects via kubectl port-forward to the AKS pod tn-heimdall/dolt-beads on port 3306. If a local dolt sql-server process is running on 127.0.0.1:3306, it will steal the port and cause 'Access denied' errors when bd commands try to connect. The start-dolt-server.ps1 script (port 3307) is a separate concern but the general pattern of starting local dolt is dangerous. AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md need an explicit prohibition section: never run dolt sql-server locally on port 3306, never run start-dolt-server.ps1 unless explicitly doing offline dev. If bd fails with Access Denied, restart kubectl port-forward: kubectl port-forward -n tn-heimdall svc/dolt-beads 3306:3306
title: Prohibit local dolt server — document in AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md

description:
Currently Forge dispatches ALL ready beads from 'bd ready --json' automatically. Add configurable control over which beads get auto-dispatched, on a per-anvil basis.

New per-anvil config fields (in AnvilConfig in internal/config/config.go):
  auto_dispatch: all | tagged | priority | off
    - all: current behavior, dispatch everything ready (default)
    - tagged: only dispatch beads whose 'tags' field contains auto_dispatch_tag
    - priority: only dispatch beads at or above auto_dispatch_min_priority
    - off: never auto-dispatch; beads only run via 'forge queue run <id>'
  auto_dispatch_tag: string  (used when auto_dispatch: tagged, e.g. 'forge-auto')
  auto_dispatch_min_priority: int  (used when auto_dispatch: priority, e.g. 1 means P0+P1 only)

Implementation notes:
- Add Tags []string json:'tags' to poller.Bead (bd ready JSON may include a tags array; if absent, tags-mode falls through to dispatch nothing).
- Add the new fields to AnvilConfig with defaults (auto_dispatch defaults to 'all').
- Apply the filter after polling in poller.Poll or in daemon.go before dispatching.
- Validate the config: if auto_dispatch=tagged, auto_dispatch_tag must be non-empty; if auto_dispatch=priority, min_priority must be 0-4.
- Update forge.yaml sample/docs with the new knobs.
- The 'off' mode is useful for repos where Forge should observe beads but never touch them without explicit human invocation.
title: Configurable per-anvil auto-dispatch behavior

description: All project documentation needs updating to reflect recently added forge.yaml settings and features. This includes CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and any files in the docs/ folder. Document: max_ci_fix_attempts (default 5), max_review_fix_attempts (default 5), max_rebase_attempts (default 3), daily_cost_limit, bellows_interval, auto_dispatch/auto_dispatch_tag per anvil, schematic_enabled, schematic_word_threshold, smith_providers vs providers distinction. Also document new Hearth panels (Ready to Merge, Events in right column layout), new CLI commands, and the changelog fragment system. Check internal/config/config.go Settings struct for the full list of configurable fields and ensure each is documented across all doc files.
title: Update all documentation with new configurable settings and features

description: When a bead fails dispatch repeatedly (claimBead fails, smith crashes on startup, etc.), Forge currently retries it every poll cycle forever. Adopt gastown's circuit breaker pattern: track consecutive dispatch failures per bead. After 3 failures, mark the bead as circuit-broken (set needs_human=1 with reason) so it stops being dispatched. This prevents one poison bead from consuming capacity every 30s. Implementation: add a dispatch_failures counter to the retries table (or a new column). In dispatchBead error paths, increment the counter. In pollAndDispatch, skip beads that have hit the threshold. Reset the counter when a human retries from the Needs Attention panel.
title: Dispatch circuit breaker: stop retrying after N consecutive failures

description:
Split the Queue panel in Hearth into three sections (or a tabbed/grouped list):

1. **Ready** — beads returned by bd ready that have the auto_dispatch_tag label (e.g. forgeReady). These are the beads Forge will pick up.
2. **Unlabeled** — beads returned by bd ready that do NOT have the auto_dispatch_tag. These are available work but not yet approved for Forge. Only show this section when auto_dispatch is set to 'tagged' in forge.yaml.
3. **In Progress** — beads with status=in_progress, showing who/what they are assigned to (worker ID or smith PID).

When the user selects a bead in the Unlabeled section and presses Enter (or a hotkey like 'l'), add the auto_dispatch_tag label to that bead via bd update <id> --add-label <tag>. This would let operators approve beads for Forge processing directly from the TUI without switching to the CLI.

Implementation notes:
- The poller already calls bd ready --json which returns beads with their labels. Filtering by tag presence is straightforward.
- For in-progress beads, query bd list --status=in_progress --json (or use the existing activeBeads sync.Map / DB workers table).
- The label action needs a new IPC command (e.g. 'tag_bead') so Hearth can ask the daemon to run bd update.
- Consider using visual separators or section headers within the existing Queue panel rather than adding more panels.
title: Hearth Queue: split into Ready/Unlabeled/In-Progress sections with label action

title: Tab panel order

description: poller.go:58 has an explicit comment: 'Poll each anvil (sequential for now; can be parallelized later)'. With multiple anvils this blocks the poll loop for N*30s worst case. Use errgroup or WaitGroup to poll all anvils concurrently, then merge results.
title: Parallelize anvil polling in BeadPoller

description: PR #6 (data.go line 182): the event fetch in FetchEvents uses a hard-coded literal 100. With wrapping, long messages now consume multiple display lines, so the effective visible event count is lower than the limit. Define a named constant (e.g., eventFetchLimit = 100) in data.go or derive the value from the panel height so the intent is clear and the value is easy to adjust.
title: Hearth: replace hard-coded event fetch limit 100 with a named constant

description:
PR #6 (hearth.go lines 566-583, 615) flags two correctness issues in wordWrap:

1. wordWrap calls strings.Fields which splits on all whitespace including newlines, collapsing repeated spaces and discarding intentional line breaks. Event log messages may contain formatted output, stack traces, or indented text that should be preserved. Fix: split on actual newlines first (wrap per paragraph), then word-wrap each paragraph independently.

2. Width decisions use len(word) (byte count) rather than display width, so Unicode/multibyte characters wrap at wrong positions. Fix: use lipgloss.Width or a runewidth helper for display-width calculations.

3. strings.TrimSpace(pStr) on every word in renderEventLines removes leading indentation from continuation lines, which corrupts formatted event output. Remove the TrimSpace or only apply it to detect blank lines.
title: Hearth: fix wordWrap to preserve newlines and use unicode-aware width

description:
Currently depcheck_interval is a global setting that applies to all anvils. Some repos (like heimdall and munin) already have bead formulas that handle dependency updates in coordinated PRs, so automatic depcheck creates duplicate noise.

Add a per-anvil `depcheck_enabled` bool pointer to AnvilConfig (following the existing pattern used by `schematic_enabled`, `golangci_lint`, and `go_race_detection`). When nil, depcheck runs as normal (opt-out). When set to false, the depcheck monitor skips that anvil entirely.

Implementation:
1. Add `DepcheckEnabled *bool` field to `AnvilConfig` in config.go
2. Update the depcheck monitor loop to check this field before scanning an anvil
3. Update docs/configuration.md with the new per-anvil setting
title: Per-anvil depcheck toggle to disable automatic dependency update scanning

description: The TUI model and data source are fully implemented in internal/hearth/ but cmd/forge/hearth.go was never created. No cobra command registers 'forge hearth', so it returns 'unknown command'. Create the command file to open the Bubbletea TUI dashboard.

description: copilotArgs() in provider.go hardcodes --model claude-sonnet-4.6 and only allows override via claude_flags. The Model field (from copilot/model-name config syntax) is never used — unlike claude and gemini providers which both honor p.Model. Fix copilotArgs() to use p.Model when set, falling back to claude-sonnet-4.6 default. This enables configs like 'copilot/gpt-5.4' or 'copilot/claude-opus-4.6' in providers/smith_providers lists.

description: This was previously fixed (Forge-rqf, Forge-6ug) but has regressed. PRs show up in Ready to Merge even though there is an outstanding review request for GitHub Copilot. Investigate why has_pending_reviews is not being set or checked correctly.

description: When a PR is created via ghpr.Create and inserted into the state DB, has_pending_reviews defaults to 0. This causes the PR to briefly appear in the Ready to Merge panel until the next bellows poll detects the pending review requests (e.g. Copilot auto-review). Fix: after inserting the PR, immediately call ghpr.CheckStatus to get reviewRequests and update has_pending_reviews in the DB before the pipeline returns. This closes the race window between PR creation and the first bellows poll.
title: Set has_pending_reviews on PR creation instead of waiting for bellows poll

description: Split the left-side Queue panel area into two stacked rows. Top row keeps the existing Queue (pending beads). Bottom row adds a new 'Needs Attention' panel showing beads with needs_human=1 or clarification_needed=1 from the retries table. Each entry should show: bead ID, anvil, title, and reason (exhausted retries, no-diff, clarification needed, collateral CI, rate-limited). Use NeedsHumanBeads() from state DB. The panel should follow the same styling conventions as the existing Queue panel (priority colors, scrollable list). Navigable via Tab like other panels.
title: Hearth: split Queue area into Queue + Needs Attention panels

description: Large or multi-part beads cause Smith to either (a) produce partial implementations, (b) exceed context limits, or (c) choose one interpretation of an ambiguous bead and get rejected by warden. Proposal: add a 'Schematic' pre-worker (fitting the forge metaphor: the schematic is drawn before the smith starts forging). The Schematic worker runs before Smith and: (1) reads the bead title+description, (2) spawns a lightweight AI session to analyse scope and propose either a decomposition into sub-beads OR a single focused implementation plan, (3) if decomposition is needed, creates the sub-beads via bd and marks the parent as blocked, (4) if the bead requires external clarification it sets clarification_needed and exits (see Forge-irf), (5) otherwise emits a focused implementation plan that is appended to Smith's prompt. Schematic is optional and gated by a size/complexity heuristic (e.g. description > N words, or an explicit 'decompose: true' tag on the bead). The forge.yaml should allow opting out per-anvil.
title: Add Schematic pre-worker to decompose large or ambiguous beads before Smith starts

description: Forge currently only supports Claude Code CLI ('claude'). When the Pro plan rate limit is exhausted, smiths fail. Implement provider fallback: 1) detect rate limit exit (claude exits with specific code or stderr message), 2) retry with Gemini CLI if available, 3) retry with GitHub Copilot CLI if Gemini unavailable. Config should allow specifying provider order and CLI command names. The smith.Spawn interface needs to be provider-agnostic or have a provider selection layer above it.
notes: Research needed before implementing: (1) What exit code and/or stderr message does 'claude' emit specifically on rate limit exhaustion vs other failures — this is needed to distinguish rate limit from real errors in smith.Spawn. (2) Whether 'gemini' CLI and 'gh copilot suggest' accept equivalent flags to claude's '-p', '--output-format stream-json', '--verbose', '--dangerously-skip-permissions' — or whether the provider layer needs to translate/adapt arguments and parse output differently per provider.
title: Fallback to Gemini then Copilot CLI when Claude rate limit hit

description:
Bellows + reviewfix have multiple interconnected bugs that prevent automatic PR fix triggering. PR #2 (Forge-f4q) demonstrates all of them.

## Bug 1 — Bellows trigger fires on wrong condition

bellows.go: NeedsChanges() only returns true for review state "CHANGES_REQUESTED".
Copilot (and many human reviewers) submit reviews as "COMMENTED" state with inline
thread suggestions. These never trigger EventReviewChanges.

Fix: Add a new prSnapshot field `HasUnresolvedThreads bool`. In checkPR, after
calling ghpr.CheckStatus, additionally call a new ghpr.FetchUnresolvedThreadCount
that uses the GraphQL reviewThreads query (see Bug 2). If unresolved thread count
transitions from 0 to >0 (or PR is newly seen with unresolved threads), emit
EventReviewChanges so reviewfix is triggered.

ghpr.CheckStatus should also be extended to include unresolved thread count alongside
the existing reviews/CI data.

## Bug 2 — fetchReviewComments uses wrong API; misses inline threads and isResolved

reviewfix.go fetchReviewComments: uses `gh pr view --json reviews,comments`.
The `comments` field in that API is PR-level general comments, NOT inline review
thread comments (which live in `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{prNumber}/comments`
or preferably via GraphQL reviewThreads). There is no isResolved field in the REST
API — only GraphQL exposes it.

Fix: Replace fetchReviewComments with a GraphQL-based implementation:

  gh api graphql -f query='
  query($owner:String!, $repo:String!, $pr:Int!) {
    repository(owner:$owner, name:$repo) {
      pullRequest(number:$pr) {
        reviewThreads(first:100) {
          nodes {
            id
            isResolved
            comments(first:1) {
              nodes { path line body author { login } }
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }' -f owner=OWNER -f repo=REPO -F pr=NUMBER

Filter nodes where isResolved: false. Map each to ReviewComment{ThreadID: node.id, ...}.
The owner/repo must be extracted from `git remote get-url origin` in the worktree.

Also fetch PR-level review body comments (non-thread) via:
  gh pr view {number} --comments
and include those that are not purely informational (heuristic: body length > 20 chars,
not an approval message).

## Bug 3 — No thread resolution after fix

After Smith successfully applies fixes and pushes, reviewfix returns without calling
the resolveReviewThread GraphQL mutation for the addressed threads.

Fix: After Smith exits with code 0, for each comment whose ThreadID is non-empty,
call:

  gh api graphql -f query='
  mutation($threadId:ID!) {
    resolveReviewThread(input:{threadId:$threadId}) {
      thread { isResolved }
    }
  }' -f threadId="{thread.id}"

Add a resolveThread(ctx, worktreePath, threadID string) error helper in reviewfix.go.
Log success/failure per thread but do not fail the overall FixResult if resolution
calls fail (thread resolution is best-effort).

## Bug 4 — CI failure event not wired to a fix worker

When bellows emits EventCIFailed, check daemon.go to confirm whether a handler
actually spawns a Smith worker to fix it. If not (likely), add a ci_fix handler
analogous to the review_fix handler: spawn Smith with a prompt listing the failing
CI checks (from gh pr checks output) and instruct it to fix build/test errors.

## Implementation order

1. Fix fetchReviewComments (Bug 2) — foundational for everything else
2. Add resolveThread after fix (Bug 3)
3. Fix bellows trigger to use unresolved thread count (Bug 1)
4. Wire EventCIFailed to a ci fix smith in daemon.go (Bug 4)

## GraphQL mutation reference (from Heimdall pr-fix skill)

Resolve thread:
  gh api graphql -f query='mutation($threadId:ID!) { resolveReviewThread(input:{threadId:$threadId}) { thread { isResolved } } }' -f threadId="THREAD_NODE_ID"

Query threads:
  gh api graphql -f query='query($owner:String!, $repo:String!, $pr:Int!) { repository(owner:$owner,name:$repo) { pullRequest(number:$pr) { reviewThreads(first:100) { nodes { id isResolved comments(first:1) { nodes { path line body author { login } } } } } } } }' -f owner=OWNER -f repo=REPO -F pr=NUMBER
title: Fix bellows+reviewfix: wrong trigger, wrong API, no thread resolution

description:
Replace the manual epic-type/label approach with automatic detection and orchestration of parent-child bead relationships. The Crucible is where separate beads are melted together into a single unified PR.

## Design

### Auto-Detection
During polling, if a bead has children (beads that list it as a dependency / it blocks other beads), the forge automatically treats it as a Crucible. No special type or label required — the structure IS the signal.

Detection: `bd show <id> --json` returns a `blocks` field listing child beads. If a ready bead has blockers, it's a Crucible candidate.

### Crucible Dispatcher (new component: `internal/crucible/`)
A new orchestration layer that manages the full lifecycle:

1. **Claim parent** — mark parent bead in_progress
2. **Create feature branch** — `feature/<parent-id>` from main, push to origin
3. **Resolve child order** — topological sort of children by their inter-dependencies. Children with no deps between them can potentially run in parallel (future optimization), but initially dispatch sequentially
4. **Dispatch child** — run the normal pipeline (Smith → Temper → Warden → PR) but targeting the feature branch instead of main
5. **Wait for child PR merge** — monitor the child's PR against the feature branch. Auto-merge when CI passes (since it's a feature branch, no human review needed). Or use bellows + existing merge flow
6. **Dispatch next child** — the feature branch now has the previous child's work, so the next Smith starts from a complete codebase
7. **Final PR** — when all children are done, create PR from feature branch → main. This gets the full review/CI treatment
8. **Close parent** — after the final PR merges (detected by bellows), close the parent bead and clean up

### Events
The Crucible must emit events at every stage so the workflow is visible in the Events panel and logs:
- `crucible_started` — parent claimed, feature branch created, N children queued
- `crucible_child_dispatched` — child bead dispatched to Smith (attempt N of M)
- `crucible_child_pr_created` — child's PR created against feature branch
- `crucible_child_merged` — child's PR merged into feature branch
- `crucible_child_failed` — child failed (exhausted retries)
- `crucible_final_pr` — all children done, final PR created against main
- `crucible_complete` — final PR merged, parent closed
- `crucible_paused` — a child failed permanently, needs human attention

### Integration Points
- `internal/daemon/daemon.go` — during pollAndDispatch, detect parent beads and hand off to Crucible instead of normal dispatch
- `internal/poller/poller.go` — enrich beads with children/blocks info (already partially there via `bd show`)
- `internal/worktree/worktree.go` — CreateEpicBranch and BaseBranch support already exist from Forge-hu9
- `internal/pipeline/pipeline.go` — BaseBranch passthrough already exists from Forge-hu9
- `internal/ghpr/ghpr.go` — Base parameter already supported
- `internal/bellows/bellows.go` — monitor feature-branch PRs, auto-merge children

### Child PR Merge Strategy
Children PRing into the feature branch should auto-merge (squash) once CI passes — no human review needed for intermediate steps. Only the final PR to main gets full review. This could be controlled by:
- Detecting the PR base is not main/master
- A config flag: `auto_merge_epic_children: true`

### Edge Cases
- Child fails permanently (exhausts retries) → pause the Crucible, surface in Needs Attention
- New commits on main conflict with feature branch → periodic rebase of feature branch onto main (between child dispatches)
- Child has external dependencies (not just on siblings) → skip until unblocked
- Parent bead has no children yet (children created later) → re-check periodically

### Testing
- Unit tests for topological sort of children
- Unit tests for Crucible state machine (started → dispatching → waiting → next → final PR → complete)
- Integration test: mock pipeline + mock ghpr to verify full lifecycle
- Test edge cases: child failure pauses Crucible, rebase between children, external deps

### Documentation
- Update CLAUDE.md component map with Crucible
- Update CLAUDE.md data flow to show Crucible path
- Update docs/configuration.md with Crucible-related settings
- Update README.md with Crucible feature description

### Key Benefit
The user just creates beads with dependencies. The Forge sees the structure and does everything else — branch creation, ordered execution, merging, final PR, cleanup. Zero manual orchestration needed.
title: Crucible: auto-orchestrate parent beads with children on feature branches

description:
Add a GitHub Actions CI workflow at .github/workflows/ci.yml. The workflow should run on push and pull_request to main.

Jobs (all on ubuntu-latest, Go version from go.mod):
1. build - go build ./...
2. vet - go vet ./...
3. test - go test ./... (once Forge-tqb adds tests; initially just verifies compilation)
4. golangci-lint (optional, use golangci/golangci-lint-action) - catches formatting and common issues

Workflow triggers:
  on:
    push:
      branches: [main]
    pull_request:
      branches: [main]

Key requirements:
- Use actions/checkout@v4 and actions/setup-go@v5 with cache: true
- Store Go cache between runs (setup-go handles this automatically with cache: true)
- Fail fast on build/vet before running tests
- The workflow filename should be ci.yml; the workflow name "CI"
- Add a status badge to README.md once it is green

Note: There is no Makefile currently. The CI should use plain go commands, not make targets.
No release or publish step needed here - this is only for PR validation and branch protection.
title: Add GitHub Actions CI workflow

description: daemon.IsRunning() calls proc.Signal(syscall.Signal(0)) to check process liveness. On Windows this is not supported and always returns an error, so IsRunning() always returns false. Fix by using ipc.SocketExists() as the liveness check on Windows, since the named pipe is destroyed when the process dies.
title: Fix daemon.IsRunning() on Windows (Signal(0) not supported)

description:
The vulncheck scanner runs on a schedule (default 24h, first scan 30s after startup). If govulncheck is not installed, ScanAll logs vuln_scan_failed every cycle. This clutters the events panel and Needs Attention with a persistent non-actionable error.

## Current behavior
- RunScheduled fires → ScanAll → exec.LookPath('govulncheck') fails → logs vuln_scan_failed event → repeats next cycle
- No way to disable it short of setting vulncheck_interval=0

## Proposed fix
1. Check for govulncheck once at Scanner construction (New()) or first RunScheduled call
2. If not found, log a single INFO message and disable scheduled scanning (don't keep retrying)
3. Add govulncheck to `forge doctor` checks as an optional dependency
4. Consider: auto-install via `go install golang.org/x/vuln/cmd/govulncheck@latest` if Go is available, or at least surface the install command prominently once
5. Add a config option `vulncheck_enabled: false` to explicitly disable if not wanted
title: Vulncheck: gracefully handle missing govulncheck instead of spamming vuln_scan_failed

description:
Copilot charges per 'premium request' with model-dependent multipliers (opus 4.6 = 3x, sonnet 4.6 = 1x, haiku 4.5 = 0.33x, gpt-5.4 = 1x, gpt-5.1-codex-max = 1x, gpt-5-mini = 0x free, etc). Each smith/warden/lifecycle invocation using copilot provider counts as 1 premium request multiplied by the model's multiplier.

Implementation:
1. Add multiplier lookup table in internal/cost/ keyed by copilot model name
2. Add copilot_premium_requests table in state.db: (date TEXT, requests_used REAL, request_limit INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY date). Use REAL for requests_used since multipliers can be fractional (0.33x).
3. Add config setting: copilot_daily_request_limit (int, default 0 = no limit). When set and exceeded, copilot provider is skipped in the fallback chain (not rate-limit — just skip).
4. Record premium requests in pipeline after each copilot invocation
5. Daemon dispatch loop checks copilot request budget before allowing copilot provider
6. Add GetTodayCopilotRequests() and AddCopilotRequest(model, multiplier) to state.DB

Premium request multiplier reference (2026):
- claude-opus-4.6: 3x, claude-opus-4.6-fast: 30x, claude-opus-4.5: 3x
- claude-sonnet-4.6/4.5/4: 1x
- claude-haiku-4.5: 0.33x
- gpt-5.4/5.3-codex/5.2-codex/5.2/5.1-codex-max/5.1-codex/5.1: 1x
- gpt-5.1-codex-mini: 0.33x
- gpt-5-mini/gpt-4.1: 0x (free)
- gemini-3-pro-preview/2.5-pro: 1x
title: Add Copilot premium request tracking and daily limit

description:
Neither Claude nor Gemini CLIs expose a standalone quota-check command.

Claude CLI (confirmed from docs at code.claude.com/docs/en/cli-reference):
- No "claude --usage", "claude usage", or "claude api usage" subcommand exists.
- The only relevant command is "claude auth status" (shows auth status as JSON, not quota).
- Quota/rate-limit info arrives exclusively via the streaming rate_limit_event, already captured
  in smith.go as RateLimitInfo{Status string, ...}.
- The rate_limit_event payload includes a "reset_at" RFC3339 timestamp telling us when the
  limit resets. This is NOT yet stored in RateLimitInfo (only Status is kept).
- Implementation: add ResetAt string to RateLimitInfo, populate from rate_limit_info.reset_at
  in readStreamJSON, surface in forge status output and hearth TUI sidebar.

Gemini CLI (confirmed from gemini-cli source and docs/resources/quota-and-pricing.md):
- No "gemini --quota", "gemini stats", or similar non-interactive quota command.
- Quota data is only available inside an active interactive session via the "/stats model"
  slash command (which calls config.getQuotaRemaining/getQuotaLimit/getQuotaResetTime).
- Quota remaining/limit/resetTime come from API response headers and are tracked in Config
  internally; there is no way to query them outside a live session.
- When Gemini hits a rate limit, the error text contains the reset time; we already detect
  this in daemon.go (rate_limit_event detection). We can parse "retry after N seconds" or
  "reset at <time>" from the error output to get a Gemini-side reset time.
- The result-event stats field (total_tokens, duration_ms etc.) does NOT include quota info.

Scoped implementation:
1. Add ResetAt string field to RateLimitInfo in smith.go.
2. Populate ResetAt from rate_limit_info.reset_at in readStreamJSON (Claude path).
3. For Gemini: attempt to parse "retry after N seconds" or RFC3339 time from ErrorOutput
   when IsRateLimited is set; store as computed reset time.
4. Persist last-seen RateLimitInfo per provider in daemon state (in-memory or state.db).
5. Include provider quota/reset info in "forge status" JSON output.
6. Show in hearth TUI: a per-provider "rate limited until <time>" indicator when relevant.
title: Show remaining rate-limit quota for Claude and Gemini

description: In Hearth TUI, the Events panel truncates event messages to a short single line, forcing the user to open log files to see what happened. description:
Create release-forge.ps1 that automates the release process. Usage: .\release-forge.ps1 0.1.0

The script should:
1. Validate the version argument (semver format)
2. Ensure working tree is clean (no uncommitted changes)
3. Ensure we are on main branch
4. Run 'forge changelog assemble' to collect changelog.d fragments into CHANGELOG.md
5. Commit the assembled CHANGELOG.md and cleared changelog.d fragments
6. Create a git tag v<version>
7. Push the commit and tag to origin
8. Print a message that the GitHub Actions release workflow will take it from here
9. Optionally open the GitHub Actions page to watch progress

This script lives in the repo and is NOT a forge subcommand — it is an operator action for repo maintainers only.
title: Release script: release-forge.ps1 for tagging and publishing releases

description:
The Warden currently reviews with a generic 5-point checklist while Copilot catches many specific patterns (data races, width inconsistencies, error swallowing, etc). Build a learning system so the Warden accumulates domain-specific review rules over time.

## Design

### 1. Warden Rules File
Per-anvil file at `.forge/warden-rules.yaml` containing learned review patterns:

```yaml
rules:
  - id: race-ctx-field
    category: concurrency
    pattern: "Plain struct field accessed from multiple goroutines"
    check: "Verify context/cancel fields use atomic.Value or are set before goroutines start"
    source: "copilot:PR#65"
    added: 2026-03-07
  - id: panel-width-consistency
    category: ui
    pattern: "Panel width calculations in TUI code"
    check: "Ensure all panels in the same row use the same width accounting formula"
    source: "copilot:PR#65"
    added: 2026-03-07
```

### 2. Warden Prompt Integration
In `buildReviewPrompt()` (internal/warden/warden.go), read `.forge/warden-rules.yaml` from the anvil path and append the rules as a numbered checklist section in the prompt. Keep it concise — each rule becomes one line like: "[ ] Check: {check} (pattern: {pattern})".

### 3. Learning Pipeline — `forge warden learn`
New subcommand that:
1. Fetches unresolved Copilot review comments from recent/specified PRs via GitHub API
2. Groups and deduplicates similar comments
3. Uses Claude to distill each unique pattern into a rule (category, pattern, check)
4. Appends to `.forge/warden-rules.yaml` with provenance (source PR)
5. Optionally runs in `--dry-run` mode to preview rules before adding

Could also run automatically: after a PR is merged, bellows triggers a learn pass on any Copilot comments from that PR.

### 4. Rule Lifecycle
- Rules accumulate in the YAML file (committed to the repo so all Forge instances share them)
- Manual pruning via editing the file or `forge warden forget <rule-id>`
- Rules that trigger false positives can be removed or refined
- Over time the Warden becomes specialized for each repo's patterns

### Key Files
- `internal/warden/warden.go` — `buildReviewPrompt()` reads rules file
- `cmd/forge/warden.go` — new `forge warden learn` subcommand
- `.forge/warden-rules.yaml` — per-anvil learned rules (committed)
title: Warden: learnable review rules from Copilot feedback

description:
Currently the Forge creates each bead's branch from main and PRs back to main. For dependent work (like the depcheck epic with 6 related beads), this breaks because child beads need code from parent beads that haven't merged yet.

## Design: Epic Branches

### Concept
An epic bead can define a `base_branch` (e.g. `feature/depcheck`). All child beads automatically:
1. Branch from the epic branch instead of main
2. Create PRs targeting the epic branch instead of main
3. Merge into the epic branch as they complete

When the epic is done, a final PR goes from the epic branch → main with all the accumulated work.

### Implementation

#### 1. Epic detection
When the Forge picks up a bead, check if it has a parent epic (via dependencies or a new `epic` field). If the parent epic has a `base_branch`, use that as the branch base instead of main.

#### 2. Worktree changes
`worktree.Create()` currently always branches from main/origin. Add a `baseBranch` parameter so epic children branch from the epic branch.

#### 3. PR targeting
`ghpr.Create()` takes a `Base` parameter (currently hardcoded to 'main'). Pass the epic branch as base for child beads.

#### 4. Epic lifecycle
- When an epic bead is picked up, create the feature branch from main (no code changes, just the branch)
- Child beads work on the epic branch
- When all children are done (all merged into epic branch), create final PR to main
- Bellows monitors the final PR normally

#### 5. Beads integration
Option A: Use bd's epic support (`bd epic`) — child beads know their parent epic
Option B: New field in forge.yaml or bead metadata: `epic_branch: feature/depcheck`
Option C: Convention — if a bead depends on another bead that has a branch, use that branch as base

### Key files to modify
- `internal/worktree/worktree.go` — add baseBranch parameter
- `internal/ghpr/ghpr.go` — CreateParams.Base already exists, just wire it
- `internal/daemon/daemon.go` — dispatch logic to detect epic children
- `internal/pipeline/pipeline.go` — pass base branch through
- `internal/poller/poller.go` — possibly surface epic info from bd

### Edge cases
- Epic branch conflicts with main (periodic rebase or merge from main)
- Child bead fails — don't block the epic, skip and continue
- Parallel children — multiple smiths working on same epic branch need coordination (sequential dispatch per epic)
title: Forge: epic/feature branch support for multi-bead work

description: In Hearth Workers panel each row shows a bead/worker entry but does not indicate which Forge component is currently active for it (Smith writing code, Temper running checks, Bellows monitoring, Warden reviewing). Fix: add a component indicator column or tag to each worker row. The worker state in the state DB tracks status (running, pending, done, failed) but not the active sub-component. Options: (1) Store the active component name in the workers table (e.g. a new phase column: smith|temper|warden|bellows|idle) and update it at each pipeline phase transition in pipeline.go and bellows.go. (2) Alternatively derive it from the event log: the most recent event for that bead ID implies the phase. Color coding: smith=yellow (writing), temper=cyan (checking), warden=magenta (reviewing), bellows=blue (monitoring).

close_reason: Fixed bd show dependents parsing in both poller/crucible.go and crucible/crucible.go
description:
The Crucible's ResolveBlocks() in poller/crucible.go calls `bd show <id> --json` and unmarshals into poller.Bead which expects `blocks []string`. But `bd show --json` returns the field as `dependents` — an array of objects with `dependency_type: 'blocks'`, not a flat string array.

Example bd show output:
```json
{
  "dependents": [
    {"id": "Forge-2cs", "title": "...", "dependency_type": "blocks"},
    {"id": "Forge-axq", "title": "...", "dependency_type": "blocks"}
  ]
}
```

But poller.Bead expects:
```go
Blocks []string `json:"blocks"`
```

Result: Blocks is always empty, IsCrucibleCandidate always returns false, and parent beads go through the normal pipeline instead of the Crucible.

## Fix
In `poller/crucible.go` lookupBlocks(), parse the `dependents` array from bd show output:

```go
type bdShowResult struct {
    Dependents []struct {
        ID             string `json:"id"`
        DependencyType string `json:"dependency_type"`
    } `json:"dependents"`
}
```

Filter for `dependency_type == "blocks"` and extract the IDs into the Blocks field.

Similarly, FetchChildren() in crucible.go line 369 reads `parent.Blocks` which will also be empty. Same fix needed there.

## Immediate Impact
Forge-m94 was dispatched as a normal bead instead of a Crucible because of this bug. Need to reset it after the fix.
title: Bug: Crucible doesn't detect parent beads — bd show returns 'dependents' not 'blocks'

description: Currently we see '● [smith] Forge-irf forge 2m37s', but I want the title of the bead there as well, so I know what the work is about. My suggestion is to use two lines, indent the second a bit and put the title there, if you don't have any other good ideas

description: Some beads are genuinely ambiguous and cannot be started without human clarification (e.g. 'which auth library?', 'should this replace X or wrap it?', 'URL of the external service?'). Currently these either fail silently (Smith produces no diff), stall in iteration loops, or exhaust all attempts uselessly. Proposed: (1) add a 'clarification_needed' flag or status that can be set via 'bd update <id> --clarification-needed' (analogous to needs_human), (2) the Needs Human Attention panel (Forge-h7x) should show these beads in a dedicated section so operators can answer and then clear the flag, (3) Forge should skip beads with clarification_needed in its poller (same as needs_human=1). For now, operators flag these manually; a future Planner worker (see Forge-yqb) may detect and set this automatically.

description:
The warden learn command (forge warden learn) currently has to be invoked manually. It should run automatically after a PR is merged — bellows already monitors merged PRs, so it's the natural place to trigger this.

After bellows detects a PR merge, it should:
1. Fetch Copilot review comments from the merged PR
2. Group and distill them into rules (if there are any)
3. Save new rules to .forge/warden-rules.yaml in the anvil
4. Create a PR with the updated rules file (similar to how depcheck creates PRs for dependency updates)
5. Log the event (e.g., warden_rule_learned)

The rules file should be checked into the repo so Warden picks it up in worktrees. The PR approach keeps it reviewable — the user can see what rules were learned and reject bad ones.

This creates a feedback loop: Copilot reviews code → Forge learns the patterns → Warden checks for them in future reviews → fewer repeated issues.
title: Auto-run warden learn after PR merges to build review rules from Copilot comments

description:
LoadAnvilConfig in temper.go returns nil for any read/unmarshal error, but docs say it returns nil only when the file doesn't exist. Permission errors, corrupt YAML, etc. are silently swallowed. It also prints errors to stderr via fmt.Fprintf instead of returning them, which is bad for daemon flows that need structured error handling.

Fix: return the error for non-os.IsNotExist cases so callers (loadAnvilTemperCached in daemon.go) can handle/log them properly. Also fix loadAnvilTemperCached to handle non-ENOENT os.Stat errors without spamming logs on every dispatch.
title: Temper: LoadAnvilConfig swallows non-ENOENT errors and prints to stderr

description:
When a Crucible is running (parent bead with children being orchestrated on a feature branch), there's no visibility into its progress in the Hearth TUI.

## Options

### Option A: Dedicated Crucible Panel
A new panel (in the left column with Queue/Ready to Merge/Needs Attention) showing:
- Active Crucibles with parent bead title
- Feature branch name
- Progress bar or fraction: 'Children: 3/6 merged'
- Current child being worked on (with Smith status)
- Status: running / paused (child failed) / final PR / complete
- Enter action: expand to show all children and their statuses

### Option B: Queue Integration
Show Crucibles as a new section in the Queue panel (like Ready/Unlabeled/In Progress):
- 'Crucible' section header
- Each Crucible shows parent title, progress, current child
- Expandable to see child list

### Recommendation
Option A (dedicated panel) is cleaner — Crucibles are a different workflow from individual beads and deserve their own space. But if panel real estate is tight, Option B works.

### Data Source
The Crucible dispatcher emits events (crucible_started, crucible_child_merged, etc.) and should also expose state via IPC so the Hearth can query:
- Active Crucibles list
- Per-Crucible: parent bead, feature branch, children with statuses, current phase
- Add a new IPC command: \ that returns this data

### Actions
- Pause/Resume a Crucible
- Skip a failed child and continue
- Retry a failed child
- View the feature branch PR
title: Hearth: Crucible panel or Queue integration to show orchestrated epic progress

description:
Add a GitHub Actions CI workflow at .github/workflows/ci.yml. The workflow should run on push and pull_request to main.

Jobs (all on ubuntu-latest, Go version from go.mod):
1. build - go build ./...
2. vet - go vet ./...
3. test - go test ./... (once Forge-tqb adds tests; initially just verifies compilation)
4. golangci-lint (optional, use golangci/golangci-lint-action) - catches formatting and common issues

Workflow triggers:
  on:
    push:
      branches: [main]
    pull_request:
      branches: [main]

Key requirements:
- Use actions/checkout@v4 and actions/setup-go@v5 with cache: true
- Store Go cache between runs (setup-go handles this automatically with cache: true)
- Fail fast on build/vet before running tests
- The workflow filename should be ci.yml; the workflow name "CI"
- Add a status badge to README.md once it is green

Note: There is no Makefile currently. The CI should use plain go commands, not make targets.
No release or publish step needed here - this is only for PR validation and branch protection.
title: Add GitHub Actions CI workflow

description:
PRs that have exhausted their CI-fix or review-fix attempt limits (ci_fix_count >= maxCI=5 or review_fix_count >= maxRev=5) silently stop being worked by Bellows with no visibility in the TUI.

Desired behaviour:
- Such PRs should appear in the Hearth TUI Needs Attention panel (left column) with a clear reason, e.g. 'CI fix exhausted (5/5)' or 'Review fix exhausted (5/5)' or 'Conflicting rebase exhausted (3/3)'.
- The panel entry should include the PR number, bead ID, anvil, and reason.
- A Retry action in the action menu should reset the counters (ci_fix_count=0, review_fix_count=0) and set status back to open, so Bellows immediately re-detects and dispatches a new fix cycle on its next poll.
- A Dismiss action should mark the PR as closed/stale so it no longer appears.

Implementation notes:
- Add a new db query (e.g. db.ExhaustedPRs()) that returns PRs with ci_fix_count >= 5 OR review_fix_count >= 5 (or matching rebase count), with status NOT IN (merged, closed).
- Extend FetchNeedsAttention in internal/hearth/data.go to include these results, mapped to NeedsAttentionItem with Reason field set.
- Use the existing action menu infrastructure in hearth.go (ActionRetry / ActionDismiss), wiring Retry to call db.UpdatePRLifecycle(id, 0, 0, ...) + db.UpdatePRStatus(id, PROpen).
- The maxCI / maxRev thresholds are currently hardcoded in lifecycle.Manager (5/5/3). Consider exposing them via config (forge.yaml settings.max_ci_fix_attempts, settings.max_review_fix_attempts) or at minimum reading from a shared constant so the DB query threshold matches.
- The listprs and resetpr tools in cmd/ are ad-hoc helpers; this feature makes them unnecessary for normal operator use.
title: Show exhausted PRs in Needs Attention panel with retry action

description:
After a CI fix worker (ActionFixCI) completes in daemon.go, there is no NotifyCIFixCompleted() call on the lifecycle manager. The lifecycle's st.CIPassing stays false and st.CINeedsFix stays true. When bellows next polls and re-detects CI failure (via its own reset snapshot), the lifecycle guard at line 267 (`if !st.CIPassing`) triggers 'already failing, skipping dispatch' and drops the event. The PR is permanently stuck — no new cifix workers are ever dispatched.

Compare with ActionFixReview (line 528) which correctly calls NotifyReviewFixCompleted() to reset ReviewNeedsFix.

## Fix
1. Add NotifyCIFixCompleted() to lifecycle Manager that resets st.CIPassing=true and st.CINeedsFix=false (mirror NotifyReviewFixCompleted logic)
2. Call it from daemon.go after the cifix worker completes (line ~491), gated on res.Error==nil
3. Also handle the DB status update: if CIPassing is reset and no ReviewNeedsFix, transition needs_fix → open so bellows can re-detect cleanly

## Reproduction
PR #76 (Forge-8rk) hit this exact bug: after retry_reset at 17:28, rebase succeeded, cifix worker ran at 17:32 and completed at 17:33, but all subsequent ci_failed events from bellows were dropped by lifecycle. PR stuck in needs_fix with ci_fix_count=1 forever.
title: Bug: CI fix worker completion doesn't reset lifecycle CIPassing flag — PR gets permanently stuck

description:
Create a new depcheck package that scans anvils for outdated dependencies across multiple ecosystems and creates beads for updates.

## Design

### Ecosystem Support
Walk each anvil directory to find project markers and scan each:
- **Go**: Find go.mod → run `go list -m -u -json all` → parse for available updates
- **.NET (NuGet)**: Find *.csproj/*.sln → run `dotnet list package --outdated --format json`
- **npm**: Find package.json (skip node_modules) → run `npm outdated --json`

Multi-project repos (e.g. Heimdall with api/ dotnet + client/ npm) are handled by discovering ALL project roots and scanning each, tagged with subdirectory path.

### Deduplication (critical)
Before creating a bead for any update, check:
1. Open/in_progress beads — `bd search` or `bd list --status=open --json` filtered for the package name
2. Open PRs — query prs table for beads referencing the package
3. Use consistent title format: `Deps(<ecosystem>): update <package> <old> → <new>` for reliable matching

### Architecture
Mirror the vulncheck pattern:
- `internal/depcheck/depcheck.go` — Scanner struct, ScanAll(), RunScheduled()
- `internal/depcheck/go.go` — Go-specific scanning
- `internal/depcheck/dotnet.go` — .NET/NuGet scanning  
- `internal/depcheck/npm.go` — npm scanning
- `internal/depcheck/dedup.go` — shared deduplication logic
- `cmd/forge/deps.go` — `forge deps check` CLI subcommand (like `forge scan`)

### Config
```yaml
settings:
  depcheck_interval: 168h  # weekly
  depcheck_enabled: true
```

Per-anvil override:
```yaml
anvils:
  heimdall:
    depcheck_enabled: false  # skip this anvil
```

### Grouping
Minor/patch updates for the same ecosystem in the same anvil should be grouped into a single bead (like the current Go updater does), not one bead per package. Major version bumps get individual beads since they likely need manual intervention.
title: Depcheck: multi-language dependency update scanner

description:
After a reviewfix worker completes and re-requests review, NeedsFix is never cleared in the lifecycle state machine. So when the reviewer fires another EventReviewChanges (second wave), the guard !st.NeedsFix ; st.Approved evaluates to false (NeedsFix=true, Approved=false) and logs 'PR changes requested again, but already in fix cycle', dropping the second wave permanently.

Root cause: lifecycle.HandleEvent for EventReviewChanges sets NeedsFix=true but only EventCIPassed clears it. Review cycles never reset NeedsFix on completion.

Fix: After ActionFixReview completes in daemon.go, call a new lifecycle method (e.g. NotifyReviewFixCompleted) that clears NeedsFix=false so the next EventReviewChanges from the re-review gets dispatched normally.
title: Bellows drops second-wave review comments after first fix cycle completes

Add a .goreleaser.yml config (similar to beads) and a release formula (.beads/formulas/forge-release.formula.toml) that: 1) assembles changelog fragments into CHANGELOG.md, 2) bumps version tag (semver), 3) runs goreleaser to build binaries. The forge version command already reads Version/Build from ldflags. Consider a 'forge release' subcommand or just use the formula with bd.
title: Release flow with GoReleaser and version tagging

description:
The crucible workflow currently requires children to have the 'parent' field set so ResolveEpicBranches can discover the epic branch. But bd ready hides children of epics (beads with parent set), so the poller never sees them — chicken-and-egg problem.

The fix: when a ready bead blocks an epic-type bead (visible in the blocks/blocked_by dependency graph), the poller should detect that relationship and resolve the epic branch from the blocked epic — without requiring the parent field. This way children stay visible in bd ready while still being routed through crucible.

Current flow: child.Parent -> lookup parent -> IsEpicBead -> resolve branch
Desired flow: child.Blocks contains epic-type bead -> resolve branch from that epic

Affected files: internal/poller/epicbranch.go, internal/poller/crucible.go, internal/daemon/daemon.go
title: Crucible: detect parent-child from blocks/blocked_by instead of requiring parent field

description: The maxCI (5), maxRev (5), and maxRebase (3) caps in lifecycle.go are hardcoded constants. They should be configurable via forge.yaml settings (e.g. settings.max_ci_fixes, settings.max_review_fixes, settings.max_rebases) so operators can tune them per deployment without code changes. Config reads in config.go, wired through daemon.go into lifecycle.New().
title: Configurable lifecycle retry caps in forge.yaml

description: Currently Forge only kills smiths that exceed smith_timeout. There is no way to distinguish a smith that is actively working from one that is hung (e.g. waiting on a blocked API call or stuck in a loop). Adopt gastown's health-state concept: periodically check smith log file modification time or process CPU usage. If a smith shows no progress for a configurable interval (e.g. 5 minutes of no log output), mark it as stalled in the workers table. The Needs Attention panel (Forge-6x7) can then surface stalled workers. Consider adding a stalled phase or status. This is different from timeout — timeout kills the process, stale detection warns the operator before the timeout fires.
title: Stale worker detection: identify stuck smiths via progress heartbeats

description: The Needs Attention panel currently shows all items identically. Enhance it to show the reason category: dispatch exhausted (circuit breaker), CI fix exhausted, review fix exhausted, rebase exhausted, clarification needed. Each category should have a distinct icon or label prefix so operators can quickly triage. This depends on Forge-wfa (exhausted lifecycle caps surfacing in Needs Attention) being implemented first.
title: Needs Attention: distinguish reason types with icons/labels

description: When pressing Retry on a bead in the Needs Attention panel, the work starts (a new worker appears), but the bead remains in the Needs Attention list. It should disappear immediately after a successful retry. The retry IPC handler in daemon.go resets the retry record (clears needs_human), but the Hearth TUI doesn't refresh the Needs Attention panel after the action completes. Either the retry handler should trigger a FetchNeedsAttention refresh, or the executeAction method in hearth.go should request a data refresh after a successful retry/dismiss action.
title: Hearth: Needs Attention item stays visible after Retry action

description: lifecycle.Manager is the central dispatch hub (CI fix, review fix, bead close, conflict detection) but has zero tests. Add table-driven tests covering: EventCIFailed dispatch, max CI attempts exhaustion, EventReviewChanges, EventPRMerged closes bead, EventPRConflicting logging, and concurrent HandleEvent calls (race detector).
title: Add tests for lifecycle.Manager state machine

description: When all providers are rate limited, pipeline.Run() fails with an 'All providers rate limited' error. The bead stays in_progress with no active worker and no way to recover without human intervention. Fix: detect this specific error in pipeline.Run() and automatically reset the bead status back to open via bd, then wait a configurable backoff period before the poller retries. Without this fix every rate-limit event leaves orphaned in_progress beads that need manual cleanup.
title: Pipeline: release bead back to open when all providers are rate limited

description:
When a PR is created, it may have automatic review requests (e.g., GitHub Copilot). Currently bellows moves the PR to the Ready to Merge panel as soon as CI passes, even if there are pending review requests that haven't been completed yet.

Bellows should check for pending reviews via the GitHub API (gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{number}/reviews or gh pr view --json reviews,reviewRequests) and only mark a PR as ready to merge when:
1. CI checks have passed
2. No review requests are pending (all requested reviews are either approved or not required)

This prevents premature merging when auto-requested reviewers like Copilot haven't finished their review yet.
title: Bellows: don't mark PR as ready-to-merge while reviews are pending

description: On daemon startup (and periodically), beads with status=in_progress but no active worker and no open PR may be orphaned (interrupted mid-session). The daemon should identify these and either reset them to open (so they can be retried) or log them for operator review. Key constraints: only touch beads belonging to THIS forge config's anvils (never touch beads from other anvil configs), and never reset a bead that has an active worker or open PR.
title: Forge: detect and recover orphaned in-progress beads on startup

description: When a bead is selected in the Needs Attention panel, allow the operator to take actions via keyboard shortcuts or a popup menu. Default action options: (1) Retry — clear needs_human flag and reset retry count, putting bead back in queue; (2) Dismiss — mark bead as resolved/won't-fix so it leaves the panel; (3) Rebase & Retry — for collateral CI failures, trigger a rebase then retry; (4) Bump Priority — change the bead's priority; (5) View Logs — show the last smith/warden log for this bead. Actions should go through the existing IPC protocol (may need new IPC commands). Consider a small popup/overlay with the action list when pressing Enter on a selected bead.
title: Hearth: interactive actions for Needs Attention beads

description:
PR #6 (line 203, hearth.go) and PR #14 (lines 226, 402, hearth.go) both flag that width/height math can go negative or overflow on small terminals.

Specific issues:
- availableHeight can be < minTop+minBottom; the current code does not degrade gracefully and can push content off-screen
- remainingWidth (m.width - 4) can go negative for very narrow terminals, yielding negative panel widths
- interiorWidth is hard-clamped to 20 even when panelWidth-4 is smaller, so wrapped lines can exceed the panel

Fix: clamp availableHeight >= 0 first, then proportionally degrade panel heights when the terminal cannot satisfy both minimums; ensure all width values are max(0, computed) before use; keep interiorWidth <= panelWidth-4 (minimum 1, not 20).
title: Hearth: clamp panel widths and heights to prevent negative dimensions on narrow terminals

description: When selecting an action for the beads in queue, and hitting enter, for example the Label bead action, it takes 3-5 seconds for the action window to close and anything to actually happen. I expect when I click enter to have immediate response
title: Queue action

description: Add a periodic check (weekly or configurable) that runs 'go list -m -u all' on each Go anvil to detect outdated dependencies. When updates are available, create a bead listing the outdated modules with current→latest versions. The Smith agent can then run 'go get -u' for compatible updates and 'go mod tidy'. Separate patch/minor updates (auto-bead) from major version bumps (needs attention). Could integrate with govulncheck to prioritize security-related updates.
title: Scheduled go module update checker

close_reason: Fixed: smith.go only treats rate_limit_event with non-warning status as a hard block; pipeline.go clears RateLimited if exit code is 0
description: Investigate and fix why smith_started happens twice for the same bead without a warden message, and why rate limit fallback triggers even when Claude isn't limited. Potential cause: smith.go incorrectly flagging errors as rate limits in IsRateLimitError or pipeline.go's provider loop logic. The events show 'Rate limit fallback to provider gemini (iteration 1)' unexpectedly.
title: Bug: Incorrect rate limit fallback and duplicated smith_started events

description:
Add unit tests for Forge's core packages. The codebase currently has no test files.

Priority packages to cover (in order):
1. internal/config - config loading, validation, defaults, poll_interval parsing, env var override
2. internal/poller - mock bd output, priority sorting, per-anvil merge, error handling
3. internal/smith - readStreamJSON: parse Claude/Gemini stream-json events, rate_limit_event, result event stats
4. internal/provider - BuildArgs output for each provider, Defaults() ordering
5. internal/ghpr - PRStatus methods (IsMerged, NeedsChanges, CIsPassing, HasApproval)
6. internal/bellows - snapshot diff logic: that events fire on transitions, not on repeat state

Test conventions:
- Use t.TempDir() for any filesystem work
- Use table-driven tests ([]struct{name, input, want}) throughout
- Mock external processes (bd, gh, claude, gemini) by swapping the exec.Command function or using a test binary approach
- No integration tests that require live network/credentials
- Place tests in the same package (_test suffix files) or a separate _test package where internal access is needed
title: Add unit tests for core packages

description: I chose the action Retry on a bead there that was in Rebase exhausted (3/3) - I got a message saying counters reset or something similar, but nothing seemed to happen. I would expect the bellow to pick it up next cycle? Some memory/cache or something that makes it still think it exhausted all it's attempts?. Investigate and fix
title: Needs Attention - Retry

description: When clicking Enter on an unlabeled bead in the Hearth TUI queue panel, an actions window pops up. Currently it only shows the available actions (Run, Clarify, etc.) without context about the bead. Display the bead's title and description in the actions popup so the user can make an informed decision without having to look up the bead separately.
title: Hearth: show bead title and description in actions popup

description:
The lifecycle manager uses a single NeedsFix boolean for both CI failures and review comments. When a CI failure sets NeedsFix=true and a review change event arrives simultaneously, the review fix is skipped with 'already in fix cycle'. When CI passes and clears NeedsFix=false, the review comments are silently forgotten because bellows only triggers on the transition from 0 to >0 unresolved threads, which already happened and was missed.

Observed on PR #57 (Forge-fv5): copilot left 6 review comments, but CI failed at the same time. CI fix was dispatched and succeeded, but review comments were never addressed.

Fix: Either (a) split NeedsFix into separate CINeedsFix and ReviewNeedsFix flags, or (b) when EventCIPassed clears NeedsFix, check if HasUnresolvedThreads is still true and re-dispatch a review fix. Option (a) is cleaner — CI and review are independent fix cycles that should not block each other.
title: Bug: lifecycle NeedsFix flag shared between CI and review causes missed review fixes

description:
When GitHub CI fails on a check that temper doesn't cover (e.g. changelog-check), the cifix worker runs temper locally, temper passes, the worker reports success — but the GitHub CI failure persists. This burns a ci_fix_count attempt for nothing.

Examples:
- PR #105: changelog-check fails on GitHub, cifix runs temper locally (build/vet/test), temper passes, nothing actually fixed
- PR #98: build-and-test fails on GitHub (possibly different env), temper passes locally

## Proposed fix
1. Before spawning cifix, fetch the actual failing check names from GitHub (already available in PRStatus.StatusCheckRollup)
2. Pass the failing check names to the cifix worker so it knows WHAT failed
3. For known checks like changelog-check: generate the missing fragment directly instead of running temper
4. For build-and-test: include the GitHub CI logs in the Smith prompt so it can fix the actual failure, not just run local checks
5. Consider: if the cifix worker's temper passes but GitHub CI is still failing, don't count it as a successful fix attempt
title: Bug: cifix worker only runs local temper — can't fix GitHub-only CI checks like changelog-check

description: we had a bunch of beads in the queue, but suddenly it says 0, with no beads in the list, even though bd ready shows 10 ready 11:04:12 poll Polled anvil: forge (10 ready)
title: Hearth: Queue appears empty

description: The Hearth TUI header ('The Forge — Hearth Dashboard') is not visible — it renders above the visible terminal area. It briefly appears when the action menu overlay pops up (Enter key), which seems to scroll the entire view down by one line, making the header momentarily visible. This happens in PowerShell on Windows. Likely a off-by-one in the total height calculation or the alt screen positioning. The header may be at row 0 which is above the viewport, or the total content height exceeds the terminal height by 1 line, pushing the header off-screen. Check the View() height math in internal/hearth/hearth.go — the sum of header + topSection + eventPanel + footer may exceed m.height. The overlay's placeOverlay function may inadvertently fix the positioning by constraining to m.height.
title: Hearth: header renders outside visible area in PowerShell terminal

description: When depcheck finds an outdated dependency but skips creating a bead because of deduplication (recently closed, open, or in-progress bead for the same package), no event is logged. Add a depcheck_skipped or depcheck_dedup event so operators can see why an update wasn't created in Hearth's event log.
title: Depcheck: log event when skipping duplicate dependency update

close_reason: Fixed: reuse existing worktree instead of erroring
description: When bellows detects CI failure, review changes, or merge conflicts, the lifecycle handler tries to create a worktree for the fix worker. But if the original pipeline's worktree wasn't cleaned up (e.g. the pipeline completed but the cleanup didn't remove the directory), the worktree.Create call returns 'worktree already exists' and the fix/rebase never runs. Fix: reuse the existing worktree instead of erroring.
title: Lifecycle actions fail when worktree already exists

description: When lifecycle.go exhausts CI fix, review fix, or rebase caps, it only logs EventLifecycleExhausted. It does NOT set needs_human=1 in the retries table, so the PR silently stops retrying without appearing in the Hearth Needs Attention panel. The lifecycle handler in daemon.go should call recordDispatchFailure or a new method to mark the PR's bead as needs_human with a reason like 'CI fix attempts exhausted (5/5)'. The Needs Attention panel should show these with a 'Retry' action that resets the relevant counter.
title: Exhausted lifecycle caps should surface in Needs Attention

description:
In the Hearth TUI, the Events panel truncates event message text to a single short
line. This forces the user to open log files manually to see what actually happened.

Fix: Increase the visible text per event row substantially. The Events panel should
show enough of the message that common cases (e.g. warden verdict, Smith summary,
CI failure reason, error text) are readable in-place.

Implementation notes:
- internal/hearth/ contains the Bubbletea TUI
- The Events panel renders rows from the state DB event log (db.ListEvents or similar)
- Increase the per-row character limit from whatever it currently is to at least 200
  characters (or ideally wrap onto a second line if the panel height allows)
- If the panel uses a fixed column width, derive the wrap width dynamically from
  the actual panel width (m.width or equivalent) so it adapts to terminal size
- For the event body/message field specifically: prefer wrapping over hard truncation
  so the user can see the full content by scrolling within the panel
- If a viewport or list component is used, ensure its height is sufficient to show
  2-3 lines per event when text wraps
- The timestamp + event type prefix can stay compact; only the message body should wrap
title: Hearth event log: show full message text instead of truncating

description: Every panel has independently reimplemented scroll logic, and each one had the same viewport bug (items disappearing when scrolling). Workers, Needs Attention, Ready to Merge, and Queue all needed the same fix (separate viewStart from selection cursor). Extract a shared scrollViewport struct/helper with methods like ScrollUp, ScrollDown, VisibleRange, etc. that all panels use. This prevents future panels from reintroducing the same bug and reduces duplicated scroll math across hearth.go.
title: Hearth: extract shared scroll viewport helper to eliminate per-panel scroll bugs

description:
After a successful merge via the Ready to Merge action, the daemon (daemon.go:1699-1704) only logs an event and returns 'ok' to the TUI. It does NOT:

1. Update the PR status in the DB to 'merged' — so ReadyToMergePRs() still returns it
2. Call bellows.Refresh() — so bellows won't re-poll until the next 2-minute interval
3. Update lifecycle state — so lifecycle still considers it active
4. Close the bead or complete workers

The PR stays visible in the Ready to Merge panel until bellows happens to poll GitHub, detects state=MERGED, and updates the DB. With the default 2-minute bellows interval, this means the PR sits there for up to 2 minutes after being merged.

## Fix
After a successful ghpr.Merge() call (line 1698), add:
1. `d.db.UpdatePRStatus(pr.ID, state.PRMerged)` — immediately remove from ReadyToMergePRs query
2. `d.bellowsMonitor.Refresh()` — trigger immediate bellows poll to handle downstream effects (bead close, worker cleanup)
3. Optionally emit a lifecycle EventPRMerged so lifecycle runs ActionCloseBead

The DB update alone would give instant feedback in the TUI. The bellows refresh ensures all downstream cleanup happens promptly rather than waiting for the next poll cycle.
title: Bug: merged PR lingers in Ready to Merge panel until next bellows poll (up to 2 min)

description:
Three improvements to the Queue panel in Hearth TUI:

1. **Close action for Unlabeled beads** — Add a 'Close' option to the action menu for beads in the Unlabeled section (which already has the 'Add label' action). This calls `bd close <id>` to close the bead directly from the TUI.

2. **Show bead title below the ID** — Like the Worker panel items which show a second line with the title, Queue items should also display the bead title on a second line. Currently they only show the bead ID which isn't very informative.

3. **Assignee on in-progress beads** — Beads in the In Progress section should show a third line with the assigned worker/user name, so the operator can see who is working on what at a glance.
title: Hearth Queue panel: add Close action, show titles, and assignee for in-progress beads

description: daemon.Stop() calls proc.Signal(syscall.SIGINT) which is not supported on Windows. Fix by adding a 'shutdown' IPC command to the daemon and using that from daemon.Stop() on Windows, with SIGINT as the Unix fallback.
title: Fix forge down on Windows (SIGINT not supported)

description: The orphan recovery logic resets any in_progress bead to open if it has no active worker in the Forge state DB. But beads can be in_progress because a human or another tool (e.g. Copilot on a colleagues machine) is working on them. The Forge should only recover beads it has a record of claiming — check the workers table or events for a bead_claimed event before resetting. Without this, adding a new anvil with existing in_progress beads causes the Forge to reset all of them to open.
title: Orphan recovery should only reset beads the Forge previously claimed

description: As events scroll through the Live Activity sub-panel, the panel grows downward, pushing the Queue and Event log panels off the screen. Root cause suspicion: renderWorkers() returns a lipgloss.JoinVertical of two sub-panels but never wraps the result in style.Height(height), so the combined height is not bounded. Also, renderWorkerActivity maxVisible calculation uses 'height - 4' but lipgloss border padding may differ, causing a few extra lines to bleed through. Fix: ensure renderWorkers clamps its total output to contentHeight, or verify that each sub-panel's style.Height() correctly clips overflow rather than expanding the box.
title: Hearth: Live Activity panel expands downward pushing other panels off screen
